Moon’s pull.<[silence:400]> Sticks and stones.<[silence:400]>

The term tide prediction comes from tracking the ocean’s rise and fall—a rhythm set by the moon’s gravity, like a silent drummer.<[silence:400]> Long before clocks or charts, coastal communities read the tides using tools carved from what washed ashore.<[silence:400]>

A Polynesian navigator tied knots in coconut fiber, each marking a high tide’s return—six knots for six hours between peaks.<[silence:400]> In 10th-century China, officials hung weighted ropes in wells, watching water levels rise as the tide pulled inland through rivers.<[silence:400]> Viking sailors counted "tide sticks"—notched driftwood planted upright on beaches—where the waterline at dawn predicted noon’s depth.<[silence:400]>

No math, just memory.<[silence:400]> A Māori proverb says:<[silence:100]> "When the pūhā flower blooms, the spring tides come." Plants, shells, even bird calls became almanacs.<[silence:400]> The tools weren’t precise by modern standards, but they turned chaos into schedule:<[silence:100]> knowing when to fish, when to beach a canoe, when to flee a storm surge.<[silence:400]>

Three related subjects are lunar calendars, indigenous navigation, and natural timekeeping.<[silence:400]>
clean_story:
Moon’s pull. Sticks and stones.

The term tide prediction comes from tracking the ocean’s rise and fall—a rhythm set by the moon’s gravity, like a silent drummer. Long before clocks or charts, coastal communities read the tides using tools carved from what washed ashore.

A Polynesian navigator tied knots in coconut fiber, each marking a high tide’s return—six knots for six hours between peaks. In 10th-century China, officials hung weighted ropes in wells, watching water levels rise as the tide pulled inland through rivers. Viking sailors counted "tide sticks"—notched driftwood planted upright on beaches—where the waterline at dawn predicted noon’s depth.

No math, just memory. A Māori proverb says: "When the pūhā flower blooms, the spring tides come." Plants, shells, even bird calls became almanacs. The tools weren’t precise by modern standards, but they turned chaos into schedule: knowing when to fish, when to beach a canoe, when to flee a storm surge.

Three related subjects are lunar calendars, indigenous navigation, and natural timekeeping.
